comment_text.sn35.82:2.3: The Pali puns lujjati (“wear away”) with loka (“world”). Normally lujjati is “fall apart, disintegrate” (see SN 22.93), but where it puns with “world” (here and SN 35.84) I translate as “wear away”, which is justified by the use at AN 3.132:1.7. This pun might seem obvious to “world-weary” folks of today, but it is the deliberate opposite of loka’s underlying sense. It originally referred to “light”, especially the bright lights of the heavens, hence “cosmos” and “world” generally. See note on MN 7:19.6 for details.

root_text.sn35.82:1.1: Atha kho aññataro bhikkhu yena bhagavā …pe… ekamantaṁ nisinno kho so bhikkhu bhagavantaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn35.82:2.2: Kittāvatā nu kho, bhante, lokoti vuccatī”ti?
root_text.sn35.82:2.6: Rūpā lujjanti, cakkhuviññāṇaṁ lujjati, cakkhusamphasso lujjati, yampidaṁ cakkhusamphassapaccayā uppajjati vedayitaṁ sukhaṁ vā dukkhaṁ vā adukkhamasukhaṁ vā tampi lujjati …pe…
root_text.sn35.82:2.8: mano lujjati, dhammā lujjanti, manoviññāṇaṁ lujjati, manosamphasso lujjati, yampidaṁ manosamphassapaccayā uppajjati vedayitaṁ sukhaṁ vā dukkhaṁ vā adukkhamasukhaṁ vā tampi lujjati.
root_text.sn35.82:2.9: Lujjatīti kho, bhikkhu, tasmā lokoti vuccatī”ti.

translation_text.sn35.82:1.1: Then a mendicant went up to the Buddha … and said to him:
translation_text.sn35.82:2.1: “Sir, they speak of this thing called ‘the world’.
translation_text.sn35.82:2.3: “It wears away, mendicant, that’s why it’s called ‘the world’.
translation_text.sn35.82:2.6: Sights … eye consciousness … eye contact is wearing away. The painful, pleasant, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on eye contact is also wearing away.
translation_text.sn35.82:2.8: The mind … ideas … mind consciousness … mind contact is wearing away. The painful, pleasant, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on mind contact is also wearing away.
translation_text.sn35.82:2.9: It wears away, mendicant, that’s why it’s called ‘the world’.”
